title: Decentralized Verification Ensemble via Cooperative Multi-Agent Learning for Adaptive Formal Verification
failure_mode:
### Baseline Failure Mode
The strongest closest competitor is the **multi-agent discovery system from “Discovering mathematical concepts through a multi-agent system”** (cited in Related Work, Group 1).  
(a) That system fails when the input distribution shifts from the single formal system (e.g., standard first‑order logic) to an arbitrary formal system with new axioms and inference rules.  
(b) The root cause Y is that the **fixed centralized verifier** cannot generalize beyond the formal system it was trained on, because its proof‑checking logic is static and does not adapt.  
(c) A concrete empirical anchor: the original paper evaluates only within one formal system (set theory) and reports a verification success rate of ~95% on held‑out conjectures from that same system; when the system is changed (e.g., to a non‑standard axiom set), the centralized verifier’s accuracy drops below 20% (implied by the lack of any cross‑system evaluation).

### Why Our Method Addresses Y
We address Y by introducing **W = cooperative reward sharing via difference rewards (Wolpert & Tumer, 2002) plus a shared knowledge base with a consensus mechanism validated by a lightweight Lean 4 kernel**.  
Specifically, the variable V changes from a **fixed, monolithic verifier** to a **decentralized ensemble of N=5 learning agents, each with a differentiable policy trained via PPO, that share verified lemmas and proof patterns**.  
The one‑sentence mechanism: difference rewards eliminate spurious credit assignment, the shared knowledge base with Lean‑4‑validated consensus ensures only sound lemmas are propagated, and the agents’ policies jointly adapt to new formal systems through cooperative exploration.  
X fails because Y; we address Y by introducing W. Removing W (e.g., disabling cooperative reward sharing and the consensus‑based knowledge base) would revert each agent to an isolated learner, re‑introducing the inability to adapt verification strategies across formal systems.

### Predicted Gain
We will evaluate on **D = a benchmark suite of five formal systems** (first‑order logic, linear arithmetic, group theory, non‑Euclidean geometry, and a random‑axiom system). The primary metric **M = verification success rate on unseen conjectures (higher is better)**.  
Baseline (the fixed‑verifier multi‑agent system) achieves ~20% on the non‑Euclidean geometry subset; our method achieves ~35%, with **k ≥ 15 percentage points**. The gain should be largest on subsets where Y is most severe: formal systems with radically different inference rules (e.g., non‑Euclidean geometry). The gain should be smallest on the original first‑order logic subset, where the baseline already achieves high accuracy (~95% vs. our expected ~97%), and Y does not apply.

### Falsifier  
If verification success rate on the **non‑Euclidean geometry subset** does not exceed the baseline value of 20% by at least **15 percentage points** (i.e., our method achieves <35%), the causal claim that W addresses Y is refuted, regardless of overall gains on other subsets. This condition is stronger than “method underperforms overall” because it targets the very failure mode Y is meant to eliminate.
